lessons.title: The Bottom Layer: Turning Bits Into a Signal
lessons.content:
You already know a network splits its work into **layers** (stacked jobs, each talking only to its neighbors). The **physical layer** is the bottom one. It has just one job, and it is wonderfully simple to picture.

Everything a computer stores is **bits** — 1s and 0s. But a 1 is just an idea; you cannot mail an idea down a wire. So the physical layer's job is to turn those bits into something that can physically *travel*, send it across the gap, and turn it back into the same bits at the other end. The 'something that travels' is called a **signal** — a physical thing the far end can detect and measure.

Think of **Morse code**: you flip a light or a buzzer on and off, and the pattern of on/off carries letters. That on/off is a signal. The physical layer does the same trick, just far faster, using one of three things: **electrical voltage** (a higher or lower 'push' of electricity) on a copper wire, **flashes of light** sent down a thin glass thread, or **radio waves** sent through the open air. At the far end, a receiver watches that signal and writes down the bits again.

The three common ways to carry a signal are called **media** (one is a *medium*): copper cable, fiber-optic, and wireless. We will meet each one next.

**Recap:** The physical layer is the bottom layer. Its only job is bits in -> a physical signal that travels (voltage, light, or radio) -> the same bits out the other side.

items.prompt: What is the one job of the physical layer?
choices: To decide which website you are visiting
To turn bits into a physical signal that can travel, then turn it back into the same bits at the far end
To store your files safely on a hard drive
To pick the fastest path across the whole internet
items.explanation: The physical layer is the bottom of the stack and does exactly one thing: it converts 1s and 0s into something physical (voltage, light, or radio waves), sends it, and converts it back to bits. Choosing websites and picking routes are jobs for higher layers; storing files is not a networking job at all.

items.prompt: In networking, what is a 'signal'?
choices: A warning message that something has gone wrong
A physical thing the far end can detect and measure, such as a voltage, a flash of light, or a radio wave
Another word for a single bit
The name of the cable that connects two computers
items.explanation: A signal is the physical thing that actually travels and carries the bits: a voltage on copper, light in fiber, or a radio wave in the air. It is not the bit itself (a bit is the idea being carried), and it is not the cable (the cable is the medium the signal travels through).

items.prompt: Why can't a computer just send the bit '1' down a wire directly?
choices: A wire can only carry the bit '0', never a '1'
A bit is just stored information; to travel it must first be turned into something physical like a voltage or a pulse of light
Wires are too slow to carry a '1'
The '1' would arrive as a '0' unless it is encrypted first
items.explanation: A bit is an abstract value, not a physical object, so it cannot move on its own. The physical layer represents it with something measurable (high vs low voltage, light on vs off, etc.). Wires carry both 1s and 0s fine, and speed or encryption is not the reason a bit needs to be turned into a signal.

items.prompt: Morse code (flipping a light on and off to spell letters) is used in this lesson as an everyday example of what idea?
choices: Turning a pattern of on/off into meaning a far-away person can read
How computers store files on disk
Why fiber is faster than copper
How the internet finds the shortest route
items.explanation: Morse code shows the core physical-layer idea: an on/off signal carries information that someone at the other end can decode. That is exactly what the physical layer does, only much faster and automatically. The other options are about different topics entirely.

items.prompt: Which three things does the physical layer commonly use to carry a signal?
choices: Passwords, files, and folders
Electrical voltage on copper, flashes of light in fiber, and radio waves in the air
Web pages, emails, and videos
Routers, switches, and firewalls
items.explanation: The three common ways to physically carry bits are voltage on copper wire, light in glass fiber, and radio waves through the air. Web pages and emails are content carried by higher layers; routers and firewalls are devices that operate above the physical layer.

items.prompt: The word 'medium' (plural 'media') in this lesson means what?
choices: A medium-sized network, between small and large
The physical path a signal travels through, such as copper cable, fiber, or air
A program that plays music and video
The middle layer of the network stack
items.explanation: A medium is simply the physical stuff a signal moves through: a copper cable, a glass fiber, or the open air for radio. It has nothing to do with size, media players, or being in the middle of the stack.

items.prompt: When the physical layer is working correctly, what should come out of the receiving end?
choices: A web page, fully drawn on the screen
The same bits that went in at the sending end
A signal that is stronger than the one that was sent
A list of every computer on the network
items.explanation: The physical layer's promise is faithful delivery of bits: the same 1s and 0s come out as went in. Drawing a web page is a much-higher-layer job, the received signal is normally weaker (not stronger), and listing computers is not a physical-layer task.

items.prompt: Where does the physical layer sit in the stack of layers?
choices: At the very top, closest to the user's app
At the very bottom, closest to the actual wire, fiber, or air
It floats between all the other layers at once
It sits outside the stack and is not really a layer
items.explanation: The physical layer is the bottom layer, right next to the real-world medium. Everything else rides on top of it. The top layers are the ones closest to the user's application; the physical layer is as far from the user and as close to the hardware as you can get.

lessons.title: Three Media, Speed, and Putting Bits on a Wave
lessons.content:
We send signals over three **media**: copper, fiber, and wireless. None is best at everything, so the choice is always a trade-off between **cost**, **distance** (how far before the signal gets too weak), **speed**, and **interference** (unwanted noise getting in).

- **Copper cable** carries electrical voltage. It is cheap and familiar (it is the cable plugged into most desk computers), but the signal fades over short distances and copper acts like an antenna, so it picks up electrical noise.
- **Fiber-optic** sends flashes of light down a hair-thin glass thread, like light through a glass pipe. It costs more and is more delicate, but it carries data the fastest, travels the farthest, and because it uses light it is immune to electrical interference.
- **Wireless / radio** sends radio waves through the open air. There is no cable to run, which is convenient, but everyone shares the same air, so it suffers the most interference and is usually slower and shorter-range than fiber.

Two words people mix up. **Bandwidth** and **bit rate** both relate to 'how fast,' but: **bit rate** is how many bits you actually push per second (measured in bits per second, *bps*) - the headline 'speed.' **Bandwidth** is the *capacity* of the medium - how much room it has to carry a signal. More bandwidth means room for a higher bit rate, the way a wider pipe can carry more water.

How do bits actually ride on a signal? Two plain ideas. **Line coding** is the rulebook for how a 1 or a 0 maps to a voltage level on a wire (for example, high voltage = 1, low voltage = 0). **Modulation** means taking a steady wave (a *carrier*) and *changing* it - making it bigger or smaller, faster or slower - so those changes spell out the bits. Either way, the receiver watches the signal and reads the bits back.

**Recap:** Copper, fiber, and wireless each trade off cost, distance, speed, and interference. Bit rate is bits-per-second; bandwidth is the capacity that allows it. Line coding maps bits to voltage levels; modulation changes a wave to carry bits.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the copper vs fiber vs wireless trade-off?
choices: Fiber is best in every way, so the other two are never used
Each medium trades off cost, distance, speed, and interference, so the best choice depends on the situation
Wireless is always the fastest because it has no cable to slow it down
Copper is immune to interference, which is why it is used everywhere
items.explanation: No single medium wins on every measure, so engineers weigh cost, distance, speed, and interference for each job. Fiber is fastest and longest-reach but pricier; wireless is convenient but the most interference-prone and usually slower; copper is cheap but short-range and noise-prone, not interference-immune.

items.prompt: Why is fiber-optic immune to electrical interference, while copper is not?
choices: Fiber carries bits as flashes of light, which electrical noise cannot disturb, while copper carries electrical voltage that nearby electrical noise can add to
Fiber is wrapped in metal shielding and copper never is
Fiber moves so fast that noise cannot keep up with it
Copper is older, and old cables always attract more noise
items.explanation: Fiber uses light, and stray electrical fields simply do not affect a beam of light, so it shrugs off electrical interference. Copper carries an electrical signal, and nearby electrical noise adds onto that signal. It is the light-vs-electricity difference, not shielding, speed, or age.

items.prompt: What is the main everyday advantage of wireless over copper and fiber?
choices: It is always faster than both
There is no cable to run, so devices can move around freely
It never suffers from interference
It can carry signals an unlimited distance
items.explanation: Wireless's big win is convenience: no physical cable, so phones and laptops can roam. It is usually slower than fiber, suffers the most interference because the air is shared, and has limited range. The freedom from cables is the real trade-off it buys.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is 'bit rate'?
choices: How many bits you actually send per second
How heavy the cable is
The number of computers on the network
How long a cable is allowed to be
items.explanation: Bit rate is the headline speed: bits pushed per second (bits per second, bps). It is not about cable weight, length, or how many machines are connected. A higher bit rate means more data moved each second.

items.prompt: The lesson compares bandwidth to a water pipe. What does 'more bandwidth' give you?
choices: A heavier cable that lasts longer
More room in the medium to carry a signal, allowing a higher bit rate
A guarantee that no bits will ever be lost
A shorter distance the signal can travel
items.explanation: Bandwidth is capacity, like the width of a pipe: a wider pipe can carry more water, and more bandwidth makes room for a higher bit rate. It does not by itself guarantee error-free delivery, change the cable's durability, or shorten the reach.

items.prompt: What does 'line coding' mean?
choices: Writing software in straight lines
The rulebook for how a 1 or a 0 maps to a voltage level on a wire
Splitting one cable into several smaller cables
Encrypting data so attackers cannot read it
items.explanation: Line coding is simply the agreed rule for representing bits as voltage levels, for example high voltage for a 1 and low voltage for a 0, so the receiver can read them back. It is not programming, cable-splitting, or encryption.

items.prompt: In plain language, what does 'modulation' mean?
choices: Deleting bits to make a message shorter
Taking a steady wave and changing it (bigger/smaller, faster/slower) so the changes spell out the bits
Turning a wireless signal into a wired one
Choosing which website to load
items.explanation: Modulation means changing a carrier wave so its variations carry data: make the wave taller for a 1 and shorter for a 0, for instance. It does not delete data, convert wireless to wired, or pick websites; those are unrelated.

items.prompt: Look at the modulation diagram: a big wave means 1 and a small wave means 0. How does the receiver recover the bits?
choices: It counts how many cables are plugged in
It measures the size of the wave in each time slot and writes down 1 for a big wave, 0 for a small one
It asks the sender to repeat every bit out loud
It guesses randomly and corrects later
items.explanation: With this kind of modulation the information is in the wave's size, so the receiver simply measures each slot: a big swing is a 1, a small swing is a 0. That is the whole idea of reading a modulated signal; no counting cables, repeating, or guessing is involved.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation best fits choosing copper over fiber?
choices: Linking two cities hundreds of kilometers apart at the highest possible speed
Connecting a desktop computer to a wall jack a few meters away, cheaply
Sending data where absolutely no electrical interference can be tolerated
A link that must run for tens of kilometers without any signal boost
items.explanation: Copper shines for short, cheap, everyday runs like a desktop to a nearby jack. Long city-to-city links, interference-free needs, and very long unboosted runs all point to fiber, which is faster, longer-reach, and immune to electrical noise.

lessons.title: Noise, Limits, and the Cables That Connect It All
lessons.content:
A signal does not travel forever unharmed. Two things wear it down. **Attenuation** means the signal gets *weaker* the farther it goes - like a voice fading as you walk away from someone. **Noise** (or **interference**) means unwanted junk gets mixed in - like trying to be heard across a loud, crowded room. Picture shouting across that room: the farther away your friend is, the fainter your voice (attenuation), and the more other chatter drowns you out (noise). Eventually the listener can no longer tell your words apart, and the receiver makes mistakes - it reads a 1 as a 0. That is why long copper runs and weak Wi-Fi get unreliable.

This leads to a key idea: every channel has a **maximum data rate**. You cannot send bits infinitely fast. The limit goes up with **more bandwidth** (more room to work in) and a **cleaner signal** (less noise compared to signal). In short: *more bandwidth plus a cleaner signal equals more bits per second*. That is the whole idea behind the famous Shannon and Nyquist results - there is a ceiling, set by how wide and how clean your channel is. (We will skip the formulas; the idea is what matters.)

Finally, the cables and connectors are standardized so gear from different makers fits together. Copper **Ethernet cable** comes in graded **categories** - you may have seen 'Cat5e,' 'Cat6,' or 'Cat8' on a cable. Higher numbers support more bandwidth and faster speeds. **Fiber** comes in two flavors: **multimode**, with a wider core, cheaper and used for shorter distances; and **single-mode**, with a very thin core that carries light the farthest, used for long hauls.

One more pair of terms about direction. A link is **half duplex** if data can flow both ways but only one direction at a time (like a walkie-talkie: you talk, then I talk). It is **full duplex** if both directions can happen at once (like a phone call where both people can speak together). Modern wired links are full duplex.

**Recap:** Attenuation weakens a signal over distance and noise corrupts it, so every channel has a max data rate - more bandwidth and a cleaner signal allow more bits. Cable categories (Cat5e/6/8) and fiber types (multimode/single-mode) are standardized, and links are half duplex (one way at a time) or full duplex (both at once).

items.prompt: What does 'attenuation' mean?
choices: A signal getting weaker the farther it travels
A signal speeding up over distance
Two cables being plugged into the same port
Bits being encrypted for safety
items.explanation: Attenuation is the natural weakening of a signal as it travels, just as a voice fades with distance. It is not about speeding up, plugging in cables, or encryption. Attenuation is a big reason links have distance limits.

items.prompt: The lesson uses 'shouting across a noisy, crowded room' to explain which idea?
choices: Why fiber costs more than copper
Signal versus noise: your fading voice is the signal, the crowd's chatter is the noise that can drown it out
How a switch learns where computers are
Why bandwidth is measured in bits per second
items.explanation: The crowded room captures signal versus noise: your voice (the signal) fades with distance (attenuation) while other chatter (noise) competes with it. When noise overwhelms the signal, the listener mishears, just as a receiver misreads bits. The other options are unrelated topics.

items.prompt: Why do signals become unreliable over long distances or with lots of interference?
choices: The bits get heavier and sink in the cable
Attenuation makes the signal faint and noise mixes in, so the receiver can no longer tell a 1 from a 0
The internet automatically slows down at night
Cables run out of bits to send
items.explanation: As distance grows the signal weakens (attenuation) and unwanted noise piles on, until the receiver cannot reliably distinguish 1s from 0s and makes errors. Bits have no weight, cables do not 'run out' of them, and time of day is not the cause.

items.prompt: As a concept (no formulas), what raises a channel's maximum data rate?
choices: More bandwidth and a cleaner signal (less noise) both let you send more bits per second
Only the brand of the cable matters
A longer cable always allows more bits per second
Lowering the bandwidth so noise has less room
items.explanation: The capacity idea behind Shannon and Nyquist is that more bandwidth (more room) and a cleaner signal (higher signal compared to noise) together raise the ceiling on bits per second. A longer cable actually hurts (more attenuation), brand is not the principle, and shrinking bandwidth lowers, not raises, the limit.

items.prompt: The Shannon/Nyquist result is best summarized for a beginner as which idea?
choices: Signals can travel infinitely fast if the cable is new
There is a ceiling on how fast a channel can carry bits, set by how wide (bandwidth) and how clean (low noise) it is
Noise always helps a signal get through
Only wireless links have a speed limit
items.explanation: The one-line takeaway is that every channel has a maximum data rate determined by its bandwidth and its noise level. Speeds are never infinite, noise always hurts, and the limit applies to all media, wired and wireless alike.

items.prompt: On an Ethernet cable you see 'Cat6' instead of 'Cat5e'. What does the higher category generally mean?
choices: It is a thinner, cheaper cable
It supports more bandwidth and faster speeds
It can only be used outdoors
It works without being plugged in
items.explanation: Cable categories are graded: a higher number like Cat6 (versus Cat5e) supports more bandwidth and therefore higher speeds. Higher categories are typically built better, not thinner-and-cheaper, are not outdoor-only, and still need to be plugged in.

items.prompt: What is the main difference between single-mode and multimode fiber?
choices: Single-mode carries electricity; multimode carries light
Single-mode has a very thin core and reaches the farthest; multimode has a wider core, is cheaper, and is used for shorter distances
Multimode is always faster and longer-range than single-mode
Single-mode is wireless and multimode is wired
items.explanation: Both are fiber and both carry light. Single-mode's very thin core sends light the farthest, ideal for long hauls; multimode's wider core is cheaper and suits shorter runs. Multimode is not the longer-range option, and neither is wireless.

items.prompt: A walkie-talkie lets you talk OR listen, but not both at the same time. Which term describes this?
choices: Full duplex
Half duplex
Attenuation
Bandwidth
items.explanation: Half duplex means data can flow both ways but only one direction at a time, exactly like a walkie-talkie. Full duplex would allow both directions at once (like a phone call). Attenuation and bandwidth describe signal strength and capacity, not direction.

items.prompt: A phone call where both people can speak and be heard at the same time is an example of what?
choices: Half duplex
Full duplex
No duplex (one-way only)
Interference
items.explanation: Full duplex means both directions can happen simultaneously, like a normal phone call. Half duplex would force one speaker at a time; one-way only would block a reply entirely. Interference is unwanted noise, not a description of direction.
